cards.reading: 「ありがとう」 と いった

meaning.en: "thank you". In order to quote someone, you append "to itta" to the sentence the person said. "itta" is past tense of "iu - to say". Note that the person who said "thank you" is not named here. It is known from context and therefore omitted as usual. If you want to name someone explicitly, you'd say: "tanaka-san ha arigatou to itta - Mr. tanaka said thank you". In Japanese, quotation marks look like this: 「」. In anime subtitles, they're usually not found, but on the following cards I added them for clarity. They have a special role, I'll comment on that again in a few cards.

cards.front: いつ私が「あなたを好きだ」と言いましたか
cards.reading: いつ わたし が 「あなた を すき だ」 と いいました か

meaning.en: When did I say: "I like you"?! -> Here the speaker is given, it's "watashi", so the speaker is quoting herself. In order to quote someone, you append "to itta" to the sentence the person said. "itta" is past tense of "iu - to say". Note that we do not have a direct quote here, because the speaker expresses that he never actually says "I like you". In this case (musing about something or "interpreted quotes" as Tae Kim calls it), the quotation marks are actually not used. I'll leave them on the following cards for clarity.

cards.front: 今日は「帰る」って言ったじゃないですか

meaning.en: Didn't say that we go home today? The "to" of "to iu / itta" is often abbreviated as "tte". Note that this only applies to this quotation context, not e.g. the conditional "to". We already had a few examples of this on earlier cards. Again, no direct quote but quotation marks for clarity - the 「」-marks are only used for real, direct quotes. Since I practice Japanese pretty much only by listening comprehension myself, I can't tell you more ;).

cards.reading: さびしい って いったら

meaning.en: If say are lonely, . So far, we had direct quotes (someone was quoted 1:1) and indirect quotes (the speaker mused about what someone might have said - the current card is another example for that). By conjugating "iu", you get even more possible meanings ("If you said" in this case). All of these have in common that you talk about what someone (might have) said. In practice, the "to" of "to iu" is often spelled "tte/って". It's easier pronounced and casual.

cards.reading: つよい って きいた けど

meaning.en: but heard that it's strong. Or: but I heard that it's strong. Instead of "iu - to say", you can also use other words, for example "kiku - to hear" or "yobu - to call". Again, all of these expressions have in common, that you talk about something that someone said. With "kiku", you express that you heard something.

cards.front: 昔私にも先生と呼ぶ人がいたの
cards.reading: むかし わたし に も せんせい と よぶ ひと が いた の

meaning.en: Long time ago, there were people who also called me "sensei". Instead of "iu - to say", you can also use other words, for example "kiku - to hear" or "yobu - to call". Again, all of these expressions have in common, that you talk about something that someone said. With "to yobu", you explain how someone is called. "to yobu" refers to "sensei" here. It's used as clause to describe "hito".

meaning.en: am called "natsume" or: call me "natsume". I hope it became clear from the previous examples that the person who's actually saying something can be omitted very often and in general can be anybody. This sentence expresses, that people in general call the guy "natsume".

cards.front: 俺はあなたを食べようとした
cards.reading: おれ は あなた を たべよう と した

meaning.en: As for me, attempted to eat you . Do you remember "tabete miru" from previous cards? The "-te miru" construct means to try out some action and see how you like it. On this card, we have a similar construction: verb volitional (you/ou-form) + to suru, which means "to attempt to do something". Tae Kim actually explained these forms together, as both mean to try something. While no effort is required for "-te miru", an effort might be required with "verb-volitional to suru".

meaning.en: give the favor of showing . miseru: to show; to display Note that this sentence never means: "show me". It's always the nature of "ageru" to go from speaker to someone else. Just in case you're wondering: It can also be used in third person, which is covered on the next card.

cards.front: それはお父さんがお母さんに買ってあげた大事なものなんだよ
cards.reading: それ は おとうさん が おかあさん に かって あげた だいじ な もの なんだ よ

meaning.en: As for that, it's a father-bought-it-for-mother-precious item (the "otousan ga okaasan ni katte ageta" describes, how "daiji" the item is: father bought it for mother). This is an example, where the speaker actually is not "-te ageru'ing" herself. Instead, her father is doing something for her mother. In this case, she and her father form a logical group or entity and from that group, something is given to someone else / outside of this group. Of course, she, her father and her mom are a group in terms of being a family, but it's the nature of "-te ageru" to never work towards the speaker, that's why I wrote: They form a "logical group".
meaning.it: As for that, it's a father-bought-it-for-mother-precious item (the "otousan ga okaasan ni katte ageta" describes, how "daiji" the item is: father bought it for mother). Of course, she, her father and her mom are a group in terms of being a family, but it's the nature of "-te ageru" to never work towards the speaker, that's why I wrote: They form a "logical group".

cards.front: お姉さんがしてあげますか
cards.reading: おねえさん が して あげます か

meaning.en: Can do it for them? Let's assume the younger girl is saying this sentence to her older sister: She is not asking "can you do it for me", because "-te ageru" never works towards the speaker. So you know that something should be done for someone else here. I.e. the two girls form one group and the "-te ageru" acts from the group towards someone outside of this group.

cards.front: 絵麻が手伝ってあげたりできないの
cards.reading: えま が てつだって あげたり できない の

meaning.en: Aren't able to give the favor of helping out? -> Can't you help them out somehow? The speaker is addressing the listener with her name here. "-te ageru" never works towards the speaker, so this sentence can't mean "can't ema help me"? Instead, imagine two people who are supposed to help someone. One of them does not want to help and says: can't you do it? Takeaway message: "-te ageru" never acts towards the speaker. This has an important influence on what is done for whom and sometimes also on who is doing something. It takes some time to get used to this.
meaning.it: Aren't able to give the favor of helping out? -> Can't you help them out somehow? The speaker is addressing the listener with her name here. meaning.it: At school, teacher gave the favor of doing a big/amazing thing. "kureru" is used when an item or favor is given to the speaker or a person close to the speaker. I.e. it represents the opposite direction of "ageru".

cards.front: でも待ってくれている人がいるから
cards.reading: でも まって くれて いる ひと が いる から

meaning.en: but there's a giving-me-favor-of-waiting-person there -> there's someone waiting for me at home. By using "kureru", the speaker is acknowledging that the person is waiting for him. "kureru" is used when an item or favor is given to the speaker or a person close to the speaker. I.e. it represents the opposite direction of "ageru".
meaning.it: but there's a giving-me-favor-of-waiting-person there -> there's someone waiting for me at home. By using "kureru", the speaker is acknowledging that the person is waiting for him. "kureru" is used when an item or favor is given to the speaker or a person close to the speaker. I.e. it represents the opposite direction of "ageru".

cards.front: じゃあ約束してくれますか
cards.reading: じゃあ やくそく して くれます か

meaning.en: Well, can give the favor of making a promise?
meaning.it: Well, can give the favor of making a promise?

cards.front: 月が出たらまた会いに来てくれるって
cards.reading: つき が でたら また あい に きて くれる って

meaning.en: If the moon comes out, gives the favor of coming again to meet. tsuki: moon "kureru" is used when an item or favor is given to the speaker or a person close to the speaker. I.e. it represents the opposite direction of "ageru".
meaning.it: If the moon comes out, gives the favor of coming again to meet. tsuki: moon "kureru" is used when an item or favor is given to the speaker or a person close to the speaker. I.e. it represents the opposite direction of "ageru".

notes.en: Ignore the "tte" in the end.
notes.it: Ignore the "tte" in the end.

cards.front: 何お母さんに作ってくれたの
cards.reading: なに おかあさん に つくって くれた の

meaning.en: Here, a mother is talking to her child. She's referring to herself in third person and says: What, you gave the favor of making this? (the kid has made some handcrafted item).
meaning.it: Here, a mother is talking to her child. She's referring to herself in third person and says: What, you gave the favor of making this? (the kid has made some handcrafted item).

meaning.en: As for today, came to give the favor of meeting you. Here, the speaker is introducing her husband to her passed away mother (they are standing in front of the mother's grave).
meaning.it: As for today, came to give the favor of meeting you. Here, the speaker is introducing her husband to her passed away mother (they are standing in front of the mother's grave).

cards.front: 去年お父さんが作ってくれたの

meaning.en: Last year, father gave the favor of making this. kyonen: last year
meaning.it: Last year, father gave the favor of making this. kyonen: last year

cards.front: 見送りに来てくれたのか
cards.reading: みおくり に きて くれた の か

meaning.en: Did come to see off? miokuri: seeing one off; farewell
meaning.it: Did come to see off? miokuri: seeing one off; farewell

cards.front: わざわざ見送りに来てくれたのか
cards.reading: わざわざ みおくり に きて くれた の か

meaning.en: Did especially come to see off? wazawaza: expressly; especially; taking the trouble (to do)
meaning.it: Did especially come to see off? wazawaza: expressly; especially; taking the trouble (to do)

cards.front: おばあちゃんが教えてくれた
cards.reading: おばあちゃん が おしえて くれた

meaning.en: Grandma gave the favor of teaching . oshieru: to teach; to instruct
meaning.it: Grandma gave the favor of teaching . oshieru: to teach; to instruct

cards.front: 案内してくれたお礼です
cards.reading: あんない して くれた おれい です

meaning.en: The adult gave something to the kid and says: the gratitude/"thank you" for giving me the favor of leading the way. orei: thanks; gratitude​ (teineigo, note the "o"-prefix)
meaning.it: The adult gave something to the kid and says: the gratitude/"thank you" for giving me the favor of leading the way. orei: thanks; gratitude​ (teineigo, note the "o"-prefix)

cards.front: まあまあそう言わないでくれる
cards.reading: まあまあ そう いわないで くれる

meaning.en: Give the favor of not saying this way. In order to ask someone to not do something, add "de" to the negative verb and add "kureru". Note that this is not the te-form.
meaning.it: Give the favor of not saying this way. In order to ask someone to not do something, add "de" to the negative verb and add "kureru". Note that this is not the te-form.

cards.front: 覚えてないなら無理しないでくれる
cards.reading: おぼえてない なら むり しないで くれる

meaning.en: If don't remember, give me the favor of not overdoing it (i.e. don't think too hard if you're not remembering it). In order to ask someone to not do something, add "de" to the negative verb and add "kureru". Note that this is not the te-form. muri: unreasonable, forced muri suru: to try too hard
meaning.it: If don't remember, give me the favor of not overdoing it (i.e. don't think too hard if you're not remembering it). cards.front: 忘れないでくれる私のこと
cards.reading: わすれないで くれる わたし の こと

meaning.en: Give me the favor of not forgetting me. In order to ask someone to not do something, add "de" to the negative verb and add "kureru". Note that this is not the te-form.
meaning.it: Give me the favor of not forgetting me. In order to ask someone to not do something, add "de" to the negative verb and add "kureru". Note that this is not the te-form.

cards.front: からかわないでくれるかな
cards.reading: からかわないで くれる か な

meaning.en: I wonder if could give the favor of not teasing me. karakau: to tease; to make fun of In order to ask someone to not do something, add "de" to the negative verb and add "kureru". Note that this is not the te-form.
meaning.it: I wonder if could give the favor of not teasing me. karakau: to tease; to make fun of In order to ask someone to not do something, add "de" to the negative verb and add "kureru". Note that this is not the te-form.

cards.front: お母さんが持ってきてくれたんだ
cards.reading: おかあさん が もって きて くれた ん だ

meaning.en: because mother was giving me the favor of bringing .
meaning.it: because mother was giving me the favor of bringing .

cards.front: お母さんが持って行ってあげるから
cards.reading: おかあさん が もって いって あげる から

meaning.en: Here, the mother is speaking and referring to herself in third person: because give you the favor of taking it with me / bringing it .
meaning.it: Here, the mother is speaking and referring to herself in third person: because give you the favor of taking it with me / bringing it .

cards.front: 俺も応援に来てやったぜ
cards.reading: おれ も おうえん に きて やった ぜ

meaning.en: I also came to support . Caution, Tae Kim writes: "Usually used for pets, animals, and such, you can substitute yaru for ageru. You would normally never use this type of yaru for people". It's often used in anime though. It's very casual, less polite and usually used to express that the receiver of the item or favor is inferior. Have a look at the references if you're interested. Remark: -te yaru has a second meaning that is less common and usually not found in any textbook. Keep this in mind for now, it's covered in about 700 cards.

cards.reading: ようい して やった ぞ さあ こい

Caution, Tae Kim writes: "Usually used for pets, animals, and such, you can substitute yaru for ageru. You would normally never use this type of yaru for people". It's often used in anime though. It's very casual, less polite and usually used to express that the receiver of the item or favor is inferior. Have a look at the references if you're interested.

cards.reading: てがみ もらった ん だ

meaning.en: because I received a letter! morau: to receive morau can be used as: wo morau. morau implies that something is received by the speaker or someone close to the speaker. If used in third person (e.g. he received), it means that "he" received something from another person, but not from the speaker. In other words, morau never means "he gets from me", so the giver is never the speaker or someone close to the speaker. This is explained very well in the book "making sense of Japanese", p. 54. Remark: This was corrected in v14, sorry for the mistake.

Remark: This was corrected in v14, sorry for the mistake. saigo: end; conclusion, also: In the end.

cards.front: いつか話してもらえるかな
cards.reading: いつか はなして もらえる か な

meaning.en: wonder if can receive talking someday... This again sounds pretty weird in English, but I try to keep the translations close to Japanese. What the speaker wants to express is: "I wonder if he will talk to me someday".
meaning.it: wonder if can receive talking someday... This again sounds pretty weird in English, but I try to keep the translations close to Japanese. What the speaker wants to express is: "I wonder if he will talk to me someday".

cards.reading: じかん を もらえる か な

meaning.en: wonder if can receive time. Note the potential form: moraeru - can receive. As you can see, "morau" is also useful when you want to ask for a favor. Usually, kureru and moraeru are used for this, Tae Kim covers this in a small section.
meaning.it: wonder if can receive time. Note the potential form: moraeru - can receive. As you can see, "morau" is also useful when you want to ask for a favor. Usually, kureru and moraeru are used for this, Tae Kim covers this in a small section.

cards.front: 許してもらえるだろうか
cards.reading: ゆるして もらえる だろう か

meaning.en: wonder if can receive forgiving. Here, forgiving is considered a favor that the speaker receives from someone. As you can see, "morau" is also useful when you want to ask for a favor. Usually, kureru and moraeru are used for this, Tae Kim covers this in a small section.

cards.front: 見せてもらえませんか
cards.reading: みせて もらえません か

meaning.en: Can't receive showing ? The negative form of "-te morau" makes asking for favors a bit more polite. Note that we have the negative potential form here (negative: moraimasen - don't receive, potential: moraeru - can receive). You could also simply ask: "misete?" but that's a lot less polite. "misete moraimasu ka" would be a better option. The negative "morau" then makes this request a bit more indirect and thus more polite.
meaning.it: Can't receive showing ? The negative form of "-te morau" makes asking for favors a bit more polite. "misete moraimasu ka" would be a better option. The negative "morau" then makes this request a bit more indirect and thus more polite.

meaning.en: because am tidying up the room. katadzukeru: to tidy up; to put in order

meaning.en: Cleaning of room, can give the favor of helping?
meaning.it: Cleaning of room, can give the favor of helping?

cards.front: ちょっと待ってください
cards.reading: ちょっと まって ください

meaning.en: Please wait a little bit. -te kudasai: Polite way of making a request (i.e. please to "verb in -te form"). Making a request this way is more direct than asking for a favor with "kureru" or "moraeru".
meaning.it: Please wait a little bit. -te kudasai: Polite way of making a request (i.e. please to "verb in -te form"). Making a request this way is more direct than asking for a favor with "kureru" or "moraeru".

cards.front: 簡単に言わないでください
cards.reading: かんたん に いわないで ください

meaning.en: Please don't say so easily. To make a negative request, use "negative verb + de kudasai", just as we learned in the context of "morau" and "kureru" before.
meaning.it: Please don't say so easily. To make a negative request, use "negative verb + de kudasai", just as we learned in the context of "morau" and "kureru" before.

cards.reading: あの これ ください

meaning.en: This, please. Just as for "ageru, kureru, morau", you can use "kudasai" for requesting both actions and items.
meaning.it: This, please. Just as for "ageru, kureru, morau", you can use "kudasai" for requesting both actions and items.

cards.front: うわあついてこないでください

If used without question, it means: I guess, I think -> more on this word later. Remark: This audio type (real world dorama) is quite hard to understand, so don't worry if you don't understand the individual words yet.

cards.reading: がんばって

meaning.en: Hang in there / do your best! In casual speech, the "kudasai" can be dropped. I think we already had a few examples of the -te form without "kudasai" on previous cards. It's sort of a soft command.
meaning.it: Hang in there / do your best! In casual speech, the "kudasai" can be dropped. I think we already had a few examples of the -te form without "kudasai" on previous cards. It's sort of a soft command.

cards.reading: ぼく と も あそんで よ

meaning.en: Also play with me ! In casual speech, the "kudasai" can be dropped. I think we already had a few examples of the -te form without "kudasai" on previous cards. It's sort of a soft command. Here, we have the "to" particle as it was originally introduced: It means "with" in this case - just in case you forgot after all the other examples.
meaning.it: Also play with me ! In casual speech, the "kudasai" can be dropped. I think we already had a few examples of the -te form without "kudasai" on previous cards. It's sort of a soft command. Here, we have the "to" particle as it was originally introduced: It means "with" in this case - just in case you forgot after all the other examples.

Side note: "nasai" is a honorific conjugation of "suru".

cards.front: さっさと食べろ置いていくぞ

meaning.en: Eat quickly! leave here. sassato: quickly (caution: rude!) Here we have the command form (taberu -> tabero). According to Tae Kim, it "is quite rude so you should use it with caution (if at all)". For -ru verbs, simply replace "ru/る" with "ro/ろ". Or vice versa: If a verb ends on "-ro", you have the command form.
meaning.it: Eat quickly! leave here. sassato: quickly (caution: rude!) Here we have the command form (taberu -> tabero). Or vice versa: If a verb ends on "-ro", you have the command form.

cards.reading: のめ よ

meaning.en: Drink! Here we have the command form (nomu -> nome). According to Tae Kim, it "is quite rude so you should use it with caution (if at all)". For -u verbs, simply replace the -u vowel sound (mu in this case) by an -e vowel sound (me in this case) to get this form. Or the sloppy listening comprehension rule: If a verb ends on ke/け, se/せ, te/て, ne/ね, me/め (or even sloppier*: if it ends on -e) and is not in -te form, you have the command form. *Why sloppier? Because the -e in the end of a verb is an artifact of the romanization and does not exist as single character. The Japanese alphabet does have an "e" character, but this one is not used here - only the ones I listed above.
meaning.it: Drink! Here we have the command form (nomu -> nome). According to Tae Kim, it "is quite rude so you should use it with caution (if at all)". For -u verbs, simply replace the -u vowel sound (mu in this case) by an -e vowel sound (me in this case) to get this form. Or the sloppy listening comprehension rule: If a verb ends on ke/け, se/せ, te/て, ne/ね, me/め (or even sloppier*: if it ends on -e) and is not in -te form, you have the command form. *Why sloppier? Because the -e in the end of a verb is an artifact of the romanization and does not exist as single character. The Japanese alphabet does have an "e" character, but this one is not used here - only the ones I listed above.

cards.reading: やめさせる

meaning.en: Make stop. yamesaseru: casuative of yameru (to stop) The causative form is the second to last verb conjugation. It's used to express "to make someone do something". For -ru verbs, replace "ru" with "saseru".
meaning.it: Make stop. yamesaseru: casuative of yameru (to stop) The causative form is the second to last verb conjugation. It's used to express "to make someone do something". For -ru verbs, replace "ru" with "saseru".

cards.reading: させる か

meaning.en: let do ? or: made do it? saseru: causative of suru Weird enough, the causative form not only means "to make someone do something", but also "to let someone do something. This really confused me, because I had only learned the causative as "make someone do something". In many cases I then wondered: Why are they forcing him to do it?! In fact, they allowed him to do it, though. Which one is actually meant must be derived from context. So: Causative: to make or to let someone do something. For -ru verbs, replace "-ru" with "-saseru".

cards.front: ピカチュウに決めさせるの
cards.reading: ぴかちゅう に きめさせる の

meaning.en: let pikachu decide? kimesaseru: causative of kimeru (to decide) Weird enough, the causative form not only means "to make someone do something", but also "to let someone do something. This really confused me, because I had only learned the causative as "make someone do something". In many cases I then wondered: Why are they forcing him to do it?! In fact, they allowed him to do it, though. Note that by letting pikachu decide, they make him do the decision, so this is where the similarity might come from. They're not forcing him though. So: Causative: to make or to let someone do something. For -ru verbs, replace "-ru" with "-saseru".

cards.front: コーヒーを入れてきます
cards.reading: こーひー を いれて きます

meaning.en: make coffee and come back. Remark 1: The expression "-te kuru" means: I go, do xxx and come back. In English, you'd probably just say: "I go and make coffee" instead of "I make coffee and come back". See ref. Remark 2: literally, "ireru" means to put in or to pour in. In case of coffee or tea, it just means to make coffee or tea. There's a podcast episode in the follow-up deck that explains this in detail ;). ko-hi-: coffee. The weird "-" marks prolong the vowels and are artifacts from romanizing katakana. This is a different Japanese writing system used for spelling loan words from other languages. In katakana, vowels are prolonged using "ー", which results in "-" in the romanization. More causative soon...

For -ru verbs, replace "-ru" with "-saseru".

cards.front: 人が立ってるのかと思った
cards.reading: ひと が たってる の か と おもった

meaning.en: because thought somebody is standing ? tatsu: to stand; to rise; to stand up​ Reminder: tatteru is short for tatte iru. The speaker was shocked and screamed, because he thought someone might be in the room. However, it was only dresses hanging there. He explains his screaming by saying this. So the "no" here is the typical explanatory one and the "ka" expresses his uncertainty by asking: Was someone there? Altogether, the sentence ending on "ka" is a clause the speaker talks about. Is it clearer now? I add this explanation to the card. It's from natsume's book of friends, s3 e5 after 5:40.

cards.reading: とめられる

meaning.en: Next we cover passive verbs, here are some good news: THIS IS THE LAST VERB CONJUGATION!!!!1 The remainder of Tae Kim's book is very easy I'd say. Hang in there! For -ru verbs, replace -ru by -rareru.

meaning.en: This is also a lie, isn't it?! deshou: it seems; I think; I guess; I wonder. When voiced as a question, it can also mean "don't you agree"? "deshou" also expresses a bit of uncertainty. However, the uncertainty is less than in case of "kamoshirenai". Keep in mind, that "uncertainty" has a large scale and does not mean someone is completely unsure. It can also be used "when someone is pretty sure something is that way, but not entirely", see ref below.

meaning.en: because it felt like someone was there. Very interesting sentence construction: dareka ita - someone was there dareka ita you - someone was there-appearance -> it appeared like someone was there ki ga suru - to have a hunch / feeling of Here, "you" is used as na-adjective to describe "ki - spirit, mood, feeling". So the speaker is suru-ing a someone-appeared-to-be-there feeling.

cards.reading: こまった らしい よ

meaning.en: Seems like in trouble. rashii: seeming ...; appearing ...​; -ish Yet another word for expressing similarity/appearance/hearsay. The difference to the others is that "rashii" is less specific. E.g. using "-sou", you draw a conclusion from something specific: You look at a dish and say: oishisou - this specific one looks delicions. You could also say: oishii rashii - you somewhere heard that something about this dish is delicious. Again, don't worry and just translate it like the others.

cards.front: あなたより僕のほうが強いので

meaning.en: I am stronger than you (in an explanatory tone). yori: sort of the opposite of "hou". It means "than" or "rather than", refers to the preceding word or clause and is often combined with "hou". The pattern or meaning is always something like this: xxx yori, yyy no hou ga zzz: yyy is more zzz than xxx. Or as Tae Kim explains it: "hou" defines the direction the comparison is leaning toward while "yori" defines the side it’s leaning away from. You don't always have to use "hou".

meaning.en: Please follow without being separated / getting disconnected. verb stem + zu: Indicates that this verb is not done while doing something else. hanareru: to be separated; to be apart; to be distant. I often mixed this up with "hanasu", if it was conjugated.

cards.reading: さむい わけ だ

meaning.en: this means / no wonder that is cold! wake (da): makes sense that / no wonder that / it means that The use of this word is a bit hard to describe: It's a noun used for making conlusions. Especially when used as "xxx wake da", it translates to what I wrote above, but it as usual depends a bit on the context.

cards.front: 誰もついてこないというわけですな
cards.reading: だれも ついて こない という わけ です な

meaning.en: no wonder that noone followed us. wake (da): makes sense that / no wonder that / it means that The use of this word is a bit hard to describe: It's a noun used for making conlusions. Especially when used as "xxx wake da", it translates to what I wrote above, but it as usual depends a bit on the context. If the "to iu" here confuses you, just ignore it for now. It kind of emphasizes what comes before, but is a bit hard to explain. Have a look at maggie sensei's elaborate explanation in the references. She answers this in detail in this article and in the comments of this article.

meaning.en: If you ignore the "ikou ka", this can mean: no wonder that lost. However, "mayou" not only means "to be lost", but also "to hesitate; to be of two minds over; to be puzzled", which actually is the correct translation here: no wonder am hesitating. Should we go? wake (da): makes sense that / no wonder that / it means that The use of this word is a bit hard to describe: It's a noun used for making conlusions. cards.front: 私はミツル君を信じるだからミツル君も私を信じて

meaning.en: I believe mitsuru-kun. Therefore, mitsuru-kun also believes me... Note the speaker's short break after "watashi ha mitsuru kun wo shinjiru". Her next sentence starts with "da kara...". Don't worry about the use of da and kara here, simply learn this as one thing: If used as conjunction (i.e. between two sentences), "da kara" means "therefore".

cards.front: どうすれば信じてくれる
cards.reading: どう すれば しんじて くれる

meaning.en: The sentence means: What should I do to make you believe? In Japanese, this is expressed very different, reminder "ba"-conditional: If A then B. Here, "A" is "dou sureba", "B" is "shinjite kureru": If do what/how/in which way, then will give me the favor of believing ?
meaning.it: The sentence means: What should I do to make you believe? In Japanese, this is expressed very different, reminder "ba"-conditional: If A then B. Here, "A" is "dou sureba", "B" is "shinjite kureru": If do what/how/in which way, then will give me the favor of believing ?

meaning.en: totally not understanding a girl's feelings! onna no ko: young woman, girl Note that parsing written Japanese is a bit inconsistent here, "onnanoko" is spelled together, while "onna no hito" had whitespaces on a previous card. It's quite some effort to correct this everywhere, so I just leave it as it is for now. Remember: Japanese has no whitespaces anyway. If spelled with kanji, this is much easier to read though: 女の子.

cards.front: さっきよりはよくなりましたよね
cards.reading: さっき より は よく なりました よ ね

meaning.en: became better than a while ago. sakki: a short while ago; a moment ago; just now; some time ago Remark: The "ha" on this card has a slightly different use than what we learned before: It is used for contrasting what comes before with what comes after. In this case, it contrasts the past (sakki yori) and the present (rest of the sentence) and and puts a certain emphasis on "sakki yori". Most important: It does not make much of a difference in nuance, if it is there or not. See refs for details. Remark 2: Actually, I am not sure if the contrasting use and the topic marking use are constructions made up by western grammar scholars, but this is just my opinion ;).
meaning.it: became better than a while ago. sakki: a short while ago; a moment ago; just now; some time ago Remark: The "ha" on this card has a slightly different use than what we learned before: It is used for contrasting what comes before with what comes after. In this case, it contrasts the past (sakki yori) and the present (rest of the sentence) and and puts a certain emphasis on "sakki yori". Most important: It does not make much of a difference in nuance, if it is there or not. See refs for details. Remark 2: Actually, I am not sure if the contrasting use and the topic marking use are constructions made up by western grammar scholars, but this is just my opinion ;).

meaning.it: Keep your eyes on shigeta's right, or more literal: Watch shigeta's right well. migi: right

cards.reading: ことば に き を つけろ

meaning.en: Watch your tone (lit: pay attention to your words). kotoba: word, phrase; but also: language / way of speaking
meaning.it: Watch your tone (lit: pay attention to your words). kotoba: word, phrase; but also: language / way of speaking

cards.front: いつまでも子供のままと思わないでください
cards.reading: いつ まで も こども の まま と おもわないで ください

meaning.en: Please don't think of me as a kid forever. kodomo: child, kid itsu made mo: forever, this is often taught as one word or phrase (itsumademo). Literally, it means something like "until even when". Reminder 1: "to omou" is used to "quote" and talk about the previous phrase in the sense: to think. It's related to "to iu". Reminder 2: mama is a noun and refers to a state. xxx no mama usually translates to "as xxx".
meaning.it: Please don't think of me as a kid forever. kodomo: child, kid itsu made mo: forever, this is often taught as one word or phrase (itsumademo). Reminder 2: mama is a noun and refers to a state. xxx no mama usually translates to "as xxx".

cards.front: それ返すためにわざわざ来たの
cards.reading: それ かえす ために わざわざ きた の

meaning.en: explicitly came here for returning that? tame ni: for; for the sake of; in order to In some resources, this is often covered as topic of its own, but not found in Tae Kim's guide. Probably, because it is just the common use case of "ni" as Tae Kim explained it: ni marks the preceding word as target of a verb or clause. In this case, the preceding word is "tame - sake, purpose".

cards.front: じゃ下の名前教えてください
cards.reading: じゃ した の なまえ おしえて ください

meaning.en: Please tell me given name. Remark: In Japanese, you usually address people with their last name. When writing down names, the last name is written first. Moreover, Japanese is traditionally written from top to bottom, so the given name is below the last name. That's why given name literally is "bottom name" in Japanese, have a look at the reference. shita: below; down; under

meaning.en: A while ago said: and went off. hashiru: to run Difficult sentence, hints: tte itte is "to iu" in it's -te form. "xxx to iu" means "to say xxx". The -te form then links it to the next part of the sentence (hashitteta), which is from hashitte ita.

cards.reading: まど から はなれてて

meaning.en: Stay away from the window...! mado: window hanareru: to be separated, to go away hanaretete: colloquial shortening of hanarete ite, ite being the -te form of "iru". So we have an enduring state here, hence the "stay away" in the translation.

meaning.en: Wait, you gotta be kidding, is there really something want to say? "masaka" is not always easy to translate directly. It is very common though and usually means, that something is unbelievable and not likely to be true. The official dictionary definition is: by no means; never!; well, I never!; you don't say!; certainly (not) Reminder: iu (to say) -> iitai (want to say)

meaning.en: Thank you. As for wanted to ask today thing, that's all -> this is all I wanted to ask you today. Reminder: kiku -> kikitai -> kikitakatta (wanted to ask)

cards.reading: ちょっと そういう いいかた は よくない よ

meaning.en: Come on, that way-of-saying is not good -> don't put it that way. souiu: such; like that; that sort of​ (refers to something close to the listener, just as for sore / kore)
meaning.it: Come on, that way-of-saying is not good -> don't put it that way. souiu: such; like that; that sort of​ (refers to something close to the listener, just as for sore / kore)

meaning.en: ...but even if win this way, it's totally not joyful/delightful -> I don't want to win like this . fuu: method; manner; way; style The pattern -te mo can be used to express: even if / regardless / no matter what.
meaning.it: ...but even if win this way, it's totally not joyful/delightful -> I don't want to win like this . fuu: method; manner; way; style The pattern -te mo can be used to express: even if / regardless / no matter what.

cards.front: これ以上なにをする気だ
cards.reading: これ いじょう なに を する き だ

meaning.en: Beyond this, what-do intention is it? This sort of means: What else do you intend to do beyond this? Do you want to cause even more trouble? ijou: beyond, more than ki: motivation, intention I'd say the major meaning of "ki" is "spirit; mind; heart​". What I found very interesting is that all other meanings such as "motivation, intention" can somehow be mapped to "spirit, mind".

cards.front: 読めない漢字があったら教えてやるぞん
cards.reading: よめない かんじ が あったら おしえて やる ぞん

meaning.en: If there's a cannot-read kanji, teach . kanji: Chinese character, which makes reading Japanese a nightmare (or fun, choose your favorite :D) Note the -te yaru construction. Here, we do not have a physical action. Instead, this is used to express the listener's lower status. A more friendly way to express this would be to use -te ageru.

cards.reading: どうしたん だ それ は

meaning.en: What happened? As for that doushitan: what happened? What's the matter? This actually is: dou shita no with the last "no" just as "n". Literally, it means: what/how was (from dou shita), the additional "no" is the explanatory one, which in a question means that an explanation is desired. Caution, this is rather informal.

cards.reading: いっしゅうかん くらい まえ か な

meaning.en: about one week ago, wasn't it? kurai: approximately, about, around (sometimes also spelled "gurai", used to make expressions or numbers more vague) isshuukan: one week Just learn isshuukan as "one week" for now. This is actually build from: ichi - one shuu - week kan - period of time I found counting boring and a nighmare for several reasons, every 5 year old Japanese kid can probably do it better than me. This will be put into a separate deck.

cards.front: どうしたのそんな暗い顔して

I already mentioned it on a different card: Japanese has many homophones (words with same pronunciation, but different meaning). They are usually written with different kanji - one of the strengths of the writing system. Since there are no kanji popping up during a conversation however, this will not help your listening comprehension much.

cards.front: 私が代わりに行ってきてやる

meaning.en: I give you (lower rank) the favor of going instead. kawari: substitute; replacement; substituting; replacing kawari ni: instead of, as substitute for Reminder: -te yaru has the meaning of -te ageru (doing something for someone directed away from the speaker), but is used for something of lower rank, e.g. when feeding your dog. When used among people, it's rather unfriendly. Here, the cat is speaking. The cat actually thinks of itself as noble being and thus adresses the protagonist as inferior.

meaning.it: That one over there is hanamoto-sensei's car, isn't it? kuruma: car, vehicle

cards.front: もしかしてあなたは人間
cards.reading: もしかして あなた は にんげん

meaning.en: By any chance, you a human? moshikashite: perhaps; possibly; maybe; by any chance; if I'm not mistaken (learn this as one word)
meaning.it: By any chance, you a human? moshikashite: perhaps; possibly; maybe; by any chance; if I'm not mistaken (learn this as one word)

cards.reading: げつようび の かお だ な

meaning.en: Monday-face, isn't it? Who doesn't know what the Monday-face is about? In this anime, the listener has a special reason to dislike mondays, I will reveal more soon ;).
meaning.it: Monday-face, isn't it? Who doesn't know what the Monday-face is about? In this anime, the listener has a special reason to dislike mondays, I will reveal more soon ;).

cards.front: それは消えてしまうわけじゃない
cards.reading: それ は きえて しまう わけ じゃない

meaning.en: As for that , it's not that disappear by themselves (the "by themselves" is a consequence of -te shimau - unintended actions). kieru: to disappear; to vanish; to go away
meaning.it: As for that , it's not that disappear by themselves (the "by themselves" is a consequence of -te shimau - unintended actions). kieru: to disappear; to vanish; to go away

cards.front: でも私にはこの記憶がない
cards.reading: でも わたし に は この きおく が ない

meaning.en: But as for me, don't have this memory. kioku: memory; recollection; remembrance
meaning.it: But as for me, don't have this memory. kioku: memory; recollection; remembrance

notes.en: This is the last card of deck version 8.
notes.it: This is the last card of deck version 8.

cards.front: なんでこんなに有名なんだ
cards.reading: なんで こんなに ゆうめい なんだ

meaning.en: Why is this famous? yuumei: famous, fame Don't confuse this with yuume - dream. The pronunciation is different.
meaning.it: Why is this famous? yuumei: famous, fame Don't confuse this with yuume - dream. The pronunciation is different.

cards.front: もう夜だしお家の人も心配するでしょうから
cards.reading: もう よる だ し おいえ の ひと も しんぱい する でしょう から

meaning.en: It's already evening and the people of her house might be worrying . yoru: evening; night
meaning.it: It's already evening and the people of her house might be worrying . yoru: evening; night

cards.reading: だめ だ たいじゅう が かるい

meaning.en: No good, body weight is light / low (they are talking about reanimating someone by cardiac massage. Obviously, you need a certain body weight for this). taijuu: (body) weight
meaning.it: No good, body weight is light / low (they are talking about reanimating someone by cardiac massage. Obviously, you need a certain body weight for this). taijuu: (body) weight

cards.front: あれお箸落としちゃった
cards.reading: あれ お はし おとしちゃった

meaning.en: Huh? accidentially dropped the chopstick hashi: chopstick
meaning.it: Huh? accidentially dropped the chopstick hashi: chopstick

notes.en: Look at the picture: What is supposed to be in her hand?
notes.it: Look at the picture: What is supposed to be in her hand?

cards.front: でも消えるってどういうこと
cards.reading: でも きえる って どういう こと

meaning.en: But as for disappearing, what kind of thing is it? -> What do you mean by "disappearing"? kieru: to disappear, to vanish
meaning.it: But as for disappearing, what kind of thing is it? -> What do you mean by "disappearing"? kieru: to disappear, to vanish

cards.reading: きょう は げつようび だろ

meaning.en: guess it's Monday today. getsuyoubi: Monday
meaning.it: guess it's Monday today. getsuyoubi: Monday
